description: The simplest and most underrated Indomie preparation. Boiled, drained, then dry fried in oil with onion and the seasoning pack until the edges crisp slightly. No vegetables, no extras — just noodles done perfectly.

ingredients.name: Indomie instant noodles
ingredients.note: Any flavour. Original or Chicken recommended for dry fry.

ingredients.name: vegetable oil
ingredients.note: Slightly more oil than stir fry — you need enough to coat and crisp the noodles.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: Thin slices that will crisp and caramelise alongside the noodles.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper
ingredients.note: Optional. Adds heat without adding moisture.

ingredients.name: indomie seasoning sachets
ingredients.note: Powder sachet only for dry fry — no liquid sachet. Liquid adds moisture and prevents crisping.

ingredients.name: egg
ingredients.note: Cracked directly into the noodles and scrambled in for a richer version.

ingredients.name: butter
ingredients.note: Added at the very end. Makes the noodles glossy and rich.

steps.title: Boil and Drain Noodles
steps.instruction: Bring a small pot of water to a boil. Add noodles — no seasoning. Boil for exactly 90 seconds — significantly undercooked. Drain thoroughly and shake the sieve to remove as much water as possible. Spread on a plate and let steam off for 1 minute. The drier the noodles, the crispier they will get in the pan.
steps.sensory_cue: Noodles should be pliable but firm — significantly undercooked compared to eating straight. They should look slightly translucent still. Surface should not be wet.
steps.failure_risk: Wet noodles steam in the pan instead of frying — you never get the crispy edges that make dry fry special. Drain very thoroughly and let them steam dry before frying.
prep_notes: Have all ingredients ready — this cooks very fast

steps.title: Fry the Onion
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan over high heat until shimmering. Add onion slices and scotch bonnet if using. Fry without stirring for 1 minute until the edges start to brown and crisp. Then stir and fry for another 30 seconds. The onion should be golden and slightly crispy — not just softened.
steps.sensory_cue: Onion edges should be golden-brown and slightly crispy. The oil should look slightly golden from the onion caramelisation. It should smell sweet and slightly smoky.
steps.failure_risk: Soft, pale onion contributes no texture to dry fry. You want some crisp and colour on the onion before the noodles go in.

steps.title: Dry Fry the Noodles
steps.instruction: Add the drained noodles to the pan. Spread them out in an even layer. Do not stir for 1 full minute — let the bottom layer make contact with the hot pan and start to crisp. Then toss and fold, spreading again. Repeat this sit-and-toss cycle 2 more times. Add the seasoning powder sachet and toss to coat evenly.
steps.sensory_cue: The noodles should develop slightly golden, crispy edges where they contact the pan. The overall texture should be drier and firmer than boiled noodles. The seasoning should coat every strand evenly.
steps.failure_risk: Constant stirring prevents any contact between the noodles and the pan surface — you never get the crispy edges. The sit-and-toss method is the technique here.

steps.title: Add Egg and Finish
steps.instruction: If using egg — push noodles to the side, crack egg in, let it set for 15 seconds then scramble and fold into the noodles immediately. Add butter if using — toss until melted. Remove from heat. Taste — the seasoning sachet should be enough but add a tiny pinch of salt if needed. Serve immediately while the edges are still crispy.
steps.sensory_cue: Finished dry fry should have a mix of textures — some strands soft, some edges slightly crispy. It should look golden and glossy. The smell is toasty, savoury, and deeply satisfying.
steps.failure_risk: The crispy texture fades quickly as the noodles sit and absorb moisture from the air. Eat immediately for the best experience.
